content:
# Leviticus Chapter 11 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 63v–64r | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 95–96

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying to them,

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: These are the living creatures that you may eat from among all the animals that are on the earth.

**3** Whatever parts the hoof and is cloven-footed and chews the cud, among the animals, that you may eat.

**4** Nevertheless, among those that chew the cud or part the hoof, you shall not eat these: the camel, because it chews the cud but does not part the hoof, is unclean to you;

**5** and the rock badger, because it chews the cud but does not part the hoof, is unclean to you;

**6** and the hare, because it chews the cud but does not part the hoof, is unclean to you;

**7** and the pig, because it parts the hoof and is cloven-footed but does not chew the cud, is unclean to you.

**8** From their flesh you shall not eat, and their carcasses you shall not touch; they are unclean to you.

**9** "These you may eat of all that are in the waters: everything in the waters that has fins and scales, whether in the seas or in the rivers, that you may eat.

**10** But everything in the seas or the rivers that does not have fins and scales, of the swarming creatures of the water and of the living creatures that are in the water, is detestable to you.

**11** They shall be detestable to you; you shall not eat their flesh, and their carcasses you shall regard as detestable.

**12** Everything in the water that does not have fins and scales is detestable to you.

**13** "And these you shall regard as detestable among the birds; they shall not be eaten — they are detestable: the eagle, the bearded vulture, the black vulture,

**14** the kite, the falcon of any kind,

**15** every raven of any kind,

**16** the ostrich, the nighthawk, the sea gull, the hawk of any kind,

**17** the little owl, the cormorant, the short-eared owl,

**18** the barn owl, the tawny owl, the carrion vulture,

**19** the stork, the heron of any kind, the hoopoe, and the bat.

**20** "All winged insects that go on all fours are detestable to you.

**21** Yet among the winged insects that go on all fours you may eat those that have jointed legs above their feet, with which to hop on the ground.

**22** Of them you may eat: the locust of any kind, the bald locust of any kind, the cricket of any kind, and the grasshopper of any kind.

**23** But all other winged insects that have four feet are detestable to you.

**24** "And by these you shall become unclean. Whoever touches their carcass shall be unclean until the evening,

**25** and whoever carries any part of their carcass shall wash his garments and be unclean until the evening.

**26** Every animal that parts the hoof but is not cloven-footed or does not chew the cud is unclean to you. Everyone who touches them shall be unclean.

**27** And all that walk on their paws, among the animals that walk on all fours, are unclean to you. Whoever touches their carcass shall be unclean until the evening,

**28** and he who carries their carcass shall wash his garments and be unclean until the evening; they are unclean to you.

**29** "And these are unclean to you among the swarming creatures that swarm on the ground: the mole rat, the mouse, the great lizard of any kind,

**30** the gecko, the monitor lizard, the lizard, the sand lizard, and the chameleon.

**31** These are unclean to you among all that swarm. Whoever touches them when they are dead shall be unclean until the evening.

**32** And anything on which any of them falls when they are dead shall be unclean, whether it is an article of wood or a garment or a skin or a sack, any article that is used for any purpose. It shall be put into water, and it shall be unclean until the evening; then it shall be clean.

**33** And if any of them falls into any earthen vessel, all that is in it shall be unclean, and you shall break it.

**34** Any food in it that could be eaten, on which water comes, shall be unclean. And any drink that could be drunk from every such vessel shall be unclean.

**35** And everything on which any part of their carcass falls shall be unclean. Whether oven or stove, it shall be broken in pieces; they are unclean and shall be unclean to you.

**36** Nevertheless, a spring or a cistern holding water shall be clean, but whoever touches a carcass in them shall be unclean.

**37** And if any part of their carcass falls upon any seed grain that is to be sown, it is clean,

**38** but if water is put on the seed and any part of their carcass falls on it, it is unclean to you.

**39** "And if any animal that you may eat dies, whoever touches its carcass shall be unclean until the evening,

**40** and whoever eats of its carcass shall wash his garments and be unclean until the evening. And whoever carries the carcass shall wash his garments and be unclean until the evening.

**41** "Every swarming creature that swarms on the ground is detestable; it shall not be eaten.

**42** Whatever goes on its belly, and whatever goes on all fours, or whatever has many feet, any swarming creature that swarms on the ground, you shall not eat, for they are detestable.

**43** You shall not make yourselves detestable with any swarming creature that swarms, and you shall not defile yourselves with them, and become unclean through them.

**44** For I am the LORD your God. Consecrate yourselves therefore, and be holy, for I am holy. You shall not defile yourselves with any swarming creature that crawls on the ground.

**45** For I am the LORD who brought you up out of the land of Egypt to be your God. You shall therefore be holy, for I am holy.

**46** This is the law about the beast and the bird and every living creature that moves through the waters and every creature that swarms on the ground,

**47** to make a distinction between the unclean and the clean and between the living creature that may be eaten and the living creature that may not be eaten."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 63v–64r.** Chapter 11 opens on folio 63v immediately following the close of chapter 10, which concluded with the resolution of the sin-offering dispute. The transition from 10:20 (vayyitav be-einav — "and he approved") to 11:1 is a new section break, marked by a parashah division in the Masoretic tradition — specifically a petuhah (open section) break, signaling a major unit transition from the narrative of priestly inauguration and its crisis (chs. 8–10) to the extended purity legislation that occupies the central chapters of Leviticus (chs. 11–15). Chapter 11 is one of the longest chapters in Leviticus (47 verses) and occupies parts of both folios 63v and 64r. The Masoretic text is internally well-structured: the chapter's repeating marker tameh hu' ("it is unclean") and the refrain sheketz hu' / sheketz hemah ("it is detestable / they are detestable") create an incantatory rhythm across the four major animal categories. The Tiberian vocalization is consistent and detailed throughout, including the careful marking of the dual criterion phrasing (mafris parsah u-shosa' shesa' ma'aleh gerah — "parts the hoof and is cloven-footed and chews the cud") and its negations for the four forbidden categories of vv. 4–7.

The formal structure of the chapter is clearly legible in the manuscript: (1) land animals — the two-criterion rule and the four named exceptions (vv. 1–8); (2) water creatures — fins and scales as the dual criterion (vv. 9–12); (3) birds — a list of forbidden species (vv. 13–19); (4) flying insects — prohibition with exception for jointed-legged hoppers (vv. 20–23); (5) carcass defilement regulations in four sub-units (vv. 24–40); (6) swarming creatures on the ground (vv. 41–43); (7) the holiness rationale and summary (vv. 44–47). The final verses (44–47) elevate the preceding list of dietary minutiae to its theological foundation: YHWH's own holiness as the ground and pattern for Israel's distinctiveness.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 95–96 (LXX).** The LXX text of Leviticus 11 presents several noteworthy divergences. The most structurally significant is the LXX's rendering of the four main categories and especially the bird list (vv. 13–19), where some species names in the MT are of disputed identification, and the LXX reflects a distinct tradition of identifications — sometimes in agreement, sometimes departing markedly. The LXX consistently renders שֶׁקֶץ (sheketz) as βδέλυγμα ("abomination, detestable thing"), a slightly more intensive lexical choice than the MT's neutral connotation of ritual taboo. The key purity vocabulary (tameh/tahor = ἀκάθαρτος/καθαρός) is consistent throughout the LXX version. Page 95 carries the bulk of the chapter; the summary material of vv. 44–47 falls on p. 96.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **Dual addressee formula: "The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron" (v. 1).** Hebrew וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל-אַהֲרֹן (va-yedabber YHWH el-Mosheh ve-el-Aharon — "and the LORD spoke to Moses and to Aaron"). This is a formal departure from the pattern of most Levitical chapters (chs. 1–7, 12, 14, etc.), where YHWH addresses Moses alone. The dual address to both Moses and Aaron reflects the specific topic of chapter 11: dietary laws that are addressed not only to Israel's mediator but to Israel's priestly family as the guardians of food-purity within the community. The dual address also frames chs. 11–15 as a block addressed to Aaron as co-legislator for the purity system. "And the LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying to them" renders the formula with full formal precision. The LXX renders Καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων λέγων πρὸς αὐτούς — in close agreement, including the explicit "to them" (πρὸς αὐτούς) for the MT's el-hem.

2. **"Living creatures" / nefesh chayyah (v. 2).** Hebrew אֶת-הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַחַיָּה (ha-nefesh ha-chayyah — "the living creature / the living soul"). The term נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh) has a wide semantic range: soul, life-force, person, creature. In the context of the dietary laws, it designates the entire category of animate living beings — the "creatures that have the breath of life" (cf. Gen 1:20–24, 2:7 where both humans and animals are called nefesh chayyah). Rendering nefesh here as "living creatures" captures the specific taxonomic use without importing the narrower English sense of "soul." "Living creatures" in v. 2 renders ha-nefesh ha-chayyah, while "living creature" in vv. 46–47 renders ha-nefesh ha-chayyah in its summary-formula use. The LXX renders τὰ ζῷα ("the animals / living beings") — using ζῷον ("living being, animal") for nefesh chayyah in its general sense.

3. **The dual criterion for clean land animals: "parts the hoof, cloven-footed, and chews the cud" (v. 3).** Hebrew מַפְרֶסֶת פַּרְסָה וְשֹׁסַעַת שֶׁסַע פְּרָסֹת מַעֲלַת גֵּרָה (mafriset parsah ve-shosa'at shesa' parsa'ot ma'alat gerah — "splitting a hoof and splitting a cleft of hooves, bringing up a cud"). The clean animal must satisfy three formally stated criteria: (a) parts the hoof (mafriset parsah — hifil participle of פָּרַס, paras, "to part, to split"); (b) is completely cloven-footed (shosa'at shesa' parsa'ot — the complete split into two halves, distinguishing fully split-hooved animals like cows and sheep from partially split or padded-paw animals); (c) chews the cud (ma'alat gerah — literally "brings up a cud," from עָלָה, 'alah, "to go up," referring to the regurgitation of partially digested food for re-chewing, the hallmark of ruminants). The triple description in the Hebrew employs two distinct terms for the hoofing criterion (paras = to split; shesa' = cleave/rift) plus the chewing criterion. The translation "parts the hoof and is cloven-footed and chews the cud" renders all three elements by compressing the two hoof-splitting expressions into one noun phrase ("cloven-footed" for shosa'at shesa' parsa'ot) following standard formal convention (ESV, NASB). The LXX renders ἀναμηρυκόμενον ("chewing the cud, ruminating") and διχηλοῦν ὁπλὴν ("parting the hoof / dividing the hoof") — in close agreement.

4. **The four named exceptions (vv. 4–7): camel, rock badger, hare, pig.** The four animals that fail one or the other of the dual criteria are named individually with explicit explanation of which criterion fails. The formal structure of each exception verse: named animal + deficient criterion + tameh hu' la-khem ("it is unclean to you"). The repetition of the phrasing is structurally deliberate — the cadence itself is part of the law's pedagogical design, making the exceptions memorable.

    - **Camel (gamal, v. 4):** Correctly noted as a cud-chewer (camelids do ruminate after a fashion) that does not have a fully split hoof (camel feet are padded, not hooved in the bovine sense). The Hebrew biological observation is accurate.
    - **Rock badger / shaphan (v. 5):** Hebrew שָׁפָן (shaphan) is the hyrax (Procavia capensis, rock hyrax) — a small mammal native to the Near East that appears to chew with a cud-like motion (coprophagy and hindgut fermentation produce a ruminant-like jaw movement) and has a vestigial inner toe rather than a properly split hoof. "Rock badger" follows the traditional English rendering (ESV, KJV: "coney/rock badger"). The LXX renders δασύπουν ("rough-footed / hare"), conflating shaphan with the hare — a notable identification error in the LXX tradition.
    - **Hare / arnevet (v. 6):** Hebrew אַרְנֶבֶת (arnevet — hare, Lepus spp.) also appears to chew its cud (hares practice cecotrophy, eating specialized droppings from the cecum, producing a cud-like jaw motion) and lacks split hooves. "The hare" follows standard formal equivalence. The LXX renders χοιρόγρυλλον ("pig-grunter / hedgehog/hyrax") — a different animal identification than the MT, possibly reflecting uncertainty about the Hebrew term or a different identification tradition.
    - **Pig / chazir (v. 7):** Hebrew חֲזִיר (chazir — pig, swine, Sus scrofa domesticus). The pig is the most culturally iconic of the four exceptions: it does split the hoof (pigs are even-toed ungulates with a split trotter) but does not chew the cud (pigs are monogastric, not ruminants). The inversion of the normal pattern — a hoof-splitter that does not ruminate, as opposed to the previous three that ruminate but don't split — makes the pig the sharpest test case of the clean/unclean taxonomy. In post-Levitical Jewish and Islamic tradition, the pig becomes the paradigmatic forbidden animal. "The pig" renders chazir precisely. The LXX renders τὸν ὗν ("the pig/swine") — in agreement.

5. **"Unclean to you" / tameh hu' la-khem (vv. 4–8, passim).** Hebrew טָמֵא הוּא לָכֶם (tameh hu' la-khem — "unclean it is to you"). The pronoun hu' ("it") functions as a copula in this predicative construction. The phrase recurs as the chapter's refrain. "Is unclean to you" renders tameh hu' la-khem precisely, preserving the copular statement. The LXX renders ἀκάθαρτόν ἐστιν ὑμῖν ("it is unclean to you") — in exact agreement. Consistent rendering: tameh = "unclean" throughout (cf. the term-rendering table established in Lev 10).

6. **"Their carcasses you shall not touch" / u-venivlatam lo' tiggau (v. 8).** Hebrew וּבְנִבְלָתָם לֹא תִגָּעוּ (u-venivlatam lo' tiggau — "and their carcasses you shall not touch"). The term נְבֵלָה (nevelah — "carcass, dead body of an animal") appears first here in chapter 11 and recurs throughout vv. 8–40 as the key term for the corpse of a forbidden (or permitted but dead) animal. The nevelah rule is crucial: touching a carcass conveys ritual impurity even for otherwise permitted animals if they died naturally (v. 39), distinguishing the clean/unclean eating taxonomy from the broader carcass-impurity taxonomy. "Carcass" renders nevelah throughout. The LXX renders τῶν θνησιμαίων ("the dead animals / the things that have died naturally") — emphasizing the natural-death dimension of nevelah.

7. **"Fins and scales" / senappir ve-qasqeset (vv. 9–12).** Hebrew סְנַפִּיר וְקַשְׂקֶשֶׂת (senappir ve-qasqeset — "fins and scales"). The dual criterion for aquatic food purity: (a) senappir — fins (from a root meaning to swim or project); (b) qasqeset — scales (from a root meaning overlapping, like a scale-pattern). Together these two features define the clean fish: any fish or aquatic creature in seas or rivers that possesses both fins and scales may be eaten; anything without both is forbidden. This definition would exclude shellfish, crustaceans, cephalopods, and scale-less fish (catfish, sharks, rays, eels). "Fins and scales" renders senappir ve-qasqeset with standard formal equivalence. The LXX renders πτερύγια καὶ λεπίδας ("fins/wings and scales") — in close agreement.

8. **"Detestable" / sheketz (vv. 10–13, 20, 23, 41–42).** Hebrew שֶׁקֶץ (sheketz) is one of the two key terms of chapter 11 alongside tameh. The root שָׁקַץ (shaqats) means to make abhorrent, to detest, to regard with loathing; the noun sheketz designates the thing that is loathsome or abominable — specifically in this chapter, aquatic creatures without fins/scales, forbidden birds, wingless/non-hopping insects, and ground-swarming creatures. The term sheketz is stronger than tameh: where tameh ("unclean") is a ritual-status term with implications for defilement-contagion but not necessarily moral revulsion, sheketz expresses active disgust and abhorrence. Sheketz is not just disqualified; it is loathsome. "Detestable" renders sheketz throughout (following ESV, NASB). Alternate English: "abominable" (KJV), "abomination" (NKJV). "Detestable" is preferred as slightly less theologically weighted than "abomination" (which in English is associated with moral sin) while capturing the disgust-connotation of the root. The related verb form (hitshaqtsutem / teshaqtsu — "you shall not make yourselves detestable / you shall not defile") is rendered correspondingly in vv. 43–44. The LXX renders βδέλυγμα ("abomination, detestable thing") — a term in Greek also used for idolatry and moral abomination, slightly more charged than the Hebrew sheketz in context.

9. **"Their carcasses you shall regard as detestable" / u-nevelatam teshaqtsevu (v. 11).** Hebrew וְאֶת-נִבְלָתָם תְּשַׁקֵּצוּ (u-nevelatam teshaqtsevu — "and their carcasses you shall detest / you shall regard as detestable"). The piel of שָׁקַץ (shaqats) is used in its causative-tolerative sense: "to cause/allow oneself to regard as detestable, to treat as loathsome." The direct object is nevelatam ("their carcasses"). "You shall regard as detestable" renders teshaqtsevu with formal precision, preserving the verbal form. The LXX renders τὰ θνησιμαῖα αὐτῶν βδελύξεσθε ("you shall regard their carcasses as abominable") — in close agreement.

10. **The bird list (vv. 13–19) — identification challenges.** The list of forbidden birds (twenty species or types, not counting "of any kind" sub-categories) presents one of the greatest identification challenges in the Hebrew Bible. Many of the bird names are hapax legomena or near-hapaxes, and the ancient versions (LXX, Peshitta, Targum Onkelos, Vulgate) frequently diverge from each other in their identifications. The Hebrew Bible does not provide a criterion for clean/unclean birds (unlike the hoof/cud and fins/scales criteria): rather, the forbidden birds are listed by name. Modern ornithological scholarship has made reasonable identifications for most of them, and this translation follows the consensus of recent critical scholarship (DCH; HALOT; Milgrom, AB Leviticus) in its renderings. The most agreed-upon identifications are used; the most disputed are flagged in the notes below.

    Key identifications:
    - **nesher (v. 13):** "eagle" — Hebrew נֶשֶׁר (nesher), rendered "eagle" following the broad consensus, though some scholars argue for "griffon vulture" (the largest Old World raptor, Gyps fulvus, which is more common in the Levant than the golden eagle). The LXX renders ἀετόν ("eagle") — in agreement with "eagle."
    - **peres (v. 13):** "bearded vulture" — Hebrew פֶּרֶס (peres), the bearded vulture (Gypaetus barbatus), which does indeed break bones (hence its name in modern Hebrew). The LXX renders γρύπα ("griffin/vulture") — a different identification.
    - **'ozniyyah (v. 13):** "black vulture" — Hebrew עָזְנִיָּה ('ozniyyah), probably the black vulture (Aegypius monachus). The LXX renders ἁλιαίετον ("sea eagle / osprey") — a markedly different identification.
    - **da'ah / raah (v. 14):** "kite" — Hebrew דָּאָה (da'ah) or רָאָה (ra'ah, the parallel text Deut 14:13 uses raah), a kite (Milvus milvus or M. migrans). The LXX renders ἰκτῖνα ("kite") — in agreement.
    - **ayyah (v. 14):** "falcon" — Hebrew אַיָּה (ayyah), a type of raptor, rendered "falcon" (Falco spp.) following modern consensus. The LXX renders γαμψώνυχα ("hook-clawed birds") — a generic rendering.
    - **'orev (v. 15):** "raven" — Hebrew עֹרֵב ('orev — raven, Corvus corax, or generically all Corvidae). "Every raven of any kind" renders kol-'orev le-minno. The LXX agrees: κόρακα ("raven/crow").
    - **bat ha-ya'anah (v. 16):** "ostrich" — Hebrew בַּת הַיַּעֲנָה (bat ha-ya'anah — "daughter of greediness / daughter of the desert"), the ostrich (Struthio camelus). The LXX renders στρουθὸν ("sparrow/ostrich") — in some manuscripts amplified to στρουθὸν τὴν μεγάλην ("the great sparrow") for the ostrich.
    - **tachmass (v. 16):** "nighthawk" — Hebrew תַּחְמָס (tachmas), possibly the nightjar (Caprimulgus europaeus) or nighthawk. LXX: γλαῦκα ("owl") — divergent.
    - **shachaf (v. 16):** "sea gull" — Hebrew שַׁחַף (shachaf). LXX: λάρον ("gull") — in agreement.
    - **nets (v. 16):** "hawk" — Hebrew נֵץ (nets), a hawk (Accipiter spp. or Buteo spp.). LXX: ἱέρακα ("hawk") — in agreement.
    - **kos (v. 17):** "little owl" — Hebrew כּוֹס (kos), the little owl (Athene noctua), the most common owl in Israel. LXX: νυκτικόρακα ("night raven/heron") — divergent.
    - **shalakh (v. 17):** "cormorant" — Hebrew שָׁלָךְ (shalakh), the plunge-diver, probably the cormorant (Phalacrocorax carbo) or gannet. LXX: καταρράκτην ("plunger/cormorant") — in agreement.
    - **yanshuf (v. 17):** "short-eared owl" — Hebrew יַנְשׁוּף (yanshuf), an owl species; "short-eared owl" (Asio flammeus) or "great owl." LXX: ἶβιν ("ibis") — a strikingly divergent identification, likely reflecting Egyptian traditions.
    - **tinshmet (v. 18):** "barn owl" — Hebrew תִּנְשֶׁמֶת (tinshemet, in its bird form), probably the barn owl (Tyto alba). Note: tinshemet appears again in v. 30 for a reptile (the chameleon), making it a homonymous term across zoological categories. LXX: πορφυρίωνα ("purple gallinule/water hen") — divergent.
    - **qa'at (v. 18):** "tawny owl" — Hebrew קָאַת (qa'at), identified variously as pelican or owl; "tawny owl" (Strix aluco) follows the majority of modern identifications for the Levantine context. LXX: πελεκᾶνα ("pelican") — divergent.
    - **racham (v. 18):** "carrion vulture" — Hebrew רָחָם (racham), the Egyptian vulture (Neophron percnopterus). LXX: πορφυρίωνα ("purple gallinule") — problematically divergent from a modern ornithological standpoint.
    - **chasidah (v. 19):** "stork" — Hebrew חֲסִידָה (chasidah — "the pious/loyal one"), the white stork (Ciconia ciconia), named for its devoted care of its young in popular tradition. LXX: ἐρωδιόν ("heron") — a related but distinct identification.
    - **anafah (v. 19):** "heron" — Hebrew אֲנָפָה (anafah), a wading bird, probably the grey heron (Ardea cinerea) or related species. LXX: χαραδριόν ("plover/stone curlew") — divergent.
    - **dukifat (v. 19):** "hoopoe" — Hebrew דּוּכִיפַת (dukifat), the hoopoe (Upupa epops), now the national bird of Israel. LXX: ἔποπα ("hoopoe") — in agreement.
    - **'atallef (v. 19):** "bat" — Hebrew עֲטַלֵּף ('atallef), the bat. The bat is classified here among birds (flying creatures), consistent with the phenomenological taxonomy of Leviticus. LXX: νυκτερίδα ("bat") — in agreement.

11. **"Winged insects that go on all fours" / kol sherets ha-'of (vv. 20–23).** Hebrew כֹּל שֶׁרֶץ הָעוֹף (kol sherets ha-'of — "every swarming thing of the fliers / every swarming flier"). The noun שֶׁרֶץ (sherets) designates swarming, teeming creatures — a category applied both to water-creatures (Gen 1:20; Lev 11:10) and to small land-crawlers (vv. 29–31, 41–42). Applied to ha-'of ("that which flies"), it designates flying insects as a class. The addition of "that go on all fours" — ha-holekh al arba' ("the walker on four") — in v. 20 characterizes flying insects as having four walking appendages (despite insects having six legs: the four main walking legs are the operative concept, the foremost pair being handled differently). "Winged insects that go on all fours" renders sherets ha-'of ha-holekh al-arba' with standard formal equivalence. The LXX renders τὰ ἑρπετὰ τῶν πετεινῶν ("the crawling things of the birds / reptiles of the flyers") — using ἑρπετόν ("creeping/crawling thing") for sherets and πετεινά ("birds/flyers") for ha-'of.

12. **"Jointed legs above their feet, with which to hop" / kenafayim le-ma'lah al regel / la-nattear ba-'arets (v. 21).** Hebrew אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ כְרָעַיִם מִמַּעַל לְרַגְלָיו לְנַתֵּר בָּהֵן עַל-הָאָרֶץ (asher lo kherayim mimma'al le-raghlav le-natter bahen al-ha-arets — "which has jumping-legs above its feet to leap with them on the ground"). The distinctive anatomical feature that marks the permitted flying insects: kherayim (dual of kehra', "leg, thigh-bone, jumping-leg" — the elongated hind femur of grasshoppers and locusts) placed above the ordinary feet (raghlav), designed for leaping (le-natter, from נָתַר, natar — "to spring, to jump, to leap"). This is a precise anatomical description of the acridid (locust/grasshopper) body plan: the large hind legs with their elongated femur and tibia form the jumping apparatus characteristic of Orthoptera. "Jointed legs above their feet, with which to hop on the ground" renders the Hebrew idiomatically; "jointed legs" renders kherayim (the thigh-joints above the feet). The LXX renders σκέλη ἀνώτερα τῶν ποδῶν αὐτοῦ ἀναπηδᾶν ἐν αὐτοῖς ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ("legs higher/above its feet to spring up with them upon the ground") — in close agreement.

13. **The four permitted insect species (v. 22).** The four named edible insects all belong to the order Orthoptera (grasshoppers, locusts, crickets): (a) arbeh — the common locust (Locusta migratoria or Schistocerca gregaria), "locust"; (b) sol'am — the bald locust, a large short-horned grasshopper, "bald locust"; (c) chargol — a cricket-like species, "cricket"; (d) chagav — the grasshopper, "grasshopper." All four are rendered with "of any kind" (le-mino — "according to its kind"), applying the four names to entire genera rather than single species. The LXX renders these as βροῦχον ("larval locust"), ἀττάκην ("locust/grasshopper"), ὀφιομάχην ("snake-fighter / large locust"), ἀκρίδα ("grasshopper/locust") — various Greek locust/grasshopper terms, not all of which map precisely to the Hebrew species names. The practice of eating locusts is well-attested in the ancient Near East and continues in various Middle Eastern and African cultures.

14. **Carcass-defilement regulations (vv. 24–40).** This extended section distinguishes between touching (ga'a — "touch, come into contact with") and carrying (nasa' — "to lift, to carry") a carcass: touching conveys impurity until evening only; carrying conveys impurity until evening and requires laundering of garments. The distinction reflects a gradation of contact-impurity (the more direct and sustained the contact, the greater the defilement transmission). The section also distinguishes between porous vessels (earthen vessels, which must be broken if defiled, v. 33) and impermeable vessels (metal or spring-fed sources, which can be purified, v. 36), and between seed in soil (not susceptible, v. 37) and moistened seed (susceptible, v. 38). "Unclean until the evening" renders tameh 'ad ha-'erev — the standard formula for the shorter impurity periods that resolve at sunset.

15. **"Spring or cistern" / ma'yan u-bor (v. 36).** Hebrew מַעְיָן וּבוֹר (ma'yan u-bor — "spring and pit/cistern"). A ma'yan is a flowing natural spring; a bor is a rock-hewn or dug cistern for water collection. The distinction matters: a spring is fed by flowing water (mayim chayyim — "living water") while a cistern collects rainwater. Together they represent gathered bodies of water used for ritual purposes. The ruling of v. 36 is that a spring or cistern is not defiled by a dead creature falling into it — only the person who touches the carcass in it becomes unclean. This may reflect a proto-halakhic principle that gathered water with sufficient volume (a mikva'ot principle) is not susceptible to corpse-impurity (later codified in Mishnah Mikva'ot). "A spring or a cistern holding water" renders ma'yan u-bor mikveh-mayim with full precision. The LXX renders πηγὴ ὑδάτων καὶ λάκκος ("a spring of waters and a cistern/pit") — in close agreement.

16. **"You shall not make yourselves detestable" / ve-lo' teshaqtsevu (v. 43).** Hebrew וְלֹא תְשַׁקְּצוּ אֶת-נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם (ve-lo' teshaqtsevu et-nafshoteikhem — "and you shall not make your souls/selves detestable"). The piel of שָׁקַץ with reflexive direct object (nafshoteikhem — "your souls/selves, your persons"): "you shall not make yourselves an object of revulsion." The use of nefesh here as the direct object intensifies the theological import: the dietary prohibitions are not merely about food or ritual status — they are about the wholeness and integrity of the person (nefesh). Defiling oneself by eating sheketz is not merely a procedural violation; it is a corruption of the self. "You shall not make yourselves detestable" renders ve-lo' teshaqtsevu et-nafshoteikhem with formal precision. The LXX renders καὶ οὐ μὴ βδελύξητε τὰς ψυχὰς ὑμῶν ("and you shall by no means make your souls an abomination") — using the strong double negative (οὐ μή) for the prohibition, and ψυχή for nefesh — in close agreement.

17. **"For I am the LORD your God. Consecrate yourselves therefore, and be holy, for I am holy" (v. 44).** This verse contains the imitatio Dei rationale for the entire chapter and, in broader scope, for the entire Levitical holiness code: Israel's holiness is grounded in and patterned after YHWH's own holiness. The formula qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheikhem ("you shall be holy, for I am holy, the LORD your God") is the programmatic statement of what scholars have called the "Holiness Code" (H), the theological heart of Leviticus 17–26 anticipated here. Three separate commands cluster in v. 44: (a) vehiqqadishtem — "and you shall consecrate/sanctify yourselves" (hithpael of קדשׁ — reflexive sanctification); (b) vihyitem qedoshim — "and you shall be holy"; (c) ki qadosh ani — "for I am holy." The pattern "be holy because I am holy" appears three times in Leviticus (11:44, 45; 19:2; 20:7, 26) and once in 1 Pet 1:16 (a NT citation of this verse). "Consecrate yourselves therefore, and be holy, for I am holy" renders the command with full formal equivalence. The LXX renders ἁγιάζεσθε καὶ ἅγιοι ἔσεσθε ὅτι ἐγὼ ἅγιος κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν ("be sanctified and you shall be holy, for I am holy — the LORD your God") — in close agreement, preserving the copular "I am holy."

18. **"The LORD who brought you up out of the land of Egypt" (v. 45).** Hebrew אֲנִי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר הֶעֱלֵיתִי אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם (ani YHWH asher he'eleti etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim — "I am the LORD who brought you up from the land of Egypt"). The verb here is הֶעֱלֵיתִי (he'eleti — hifil perfect of עָלָה, 'alah — "to bring up"). Note the deliberate choice: not hotsets'ti ("I brought you out," which appears in the Decalogue, Exod 20:2) but he'eleti ("I brought you up"). The verb 'alah is the standard verb used for the exodus in Deuteronomy and the Psalms when the perspective is the upward movement from Egypt to Canaan (theologically and geographically higher ground). The variation from the more familiar hotsets'ti of the Decalogue is notable: here YHWH is not merely the one who got Israel out but the one who brought them up, elevated them, raised them to a higher status. The exodus is thus connected directly to the holiness calling: being brought up is part of being made holy. "The LORD who brought you up out of the land of Egypt" renders ani YHWH asher he'eleti etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim precisely. The LXX renders ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος ὁ ἀναγαγών ὑμᾶς ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ("I am the LORD who brought you up out of the land of Egypt") — in agreement, using ἀνάγω ("to lead up, to bring up") for he'eleti.

19. **"This is the law / torah" (v. 46).** Hebrew זֹאת תּוֹרַת (zo't torat — "this is the law of"). The noun תּוֹרָה (torah) here is used in its pre-canonical, functional sense: an instruction, a law, a body of teaching. The phrase zo't ha-torah serves as a formal summary label for the legislative unit that has just been given — the dietary laws are here explicitly designated a torah (teaching/law) for Israel. Translating torah as "law" here is the standard formal equivalence choice (ESV, NASB, KJV), capturing the normative, legally binding force of the dietary instruction without importing the post-canonical loaded sense of "the Torah" (the Pentateuch) or "the Law" (in the NT antithesis). "This is the law" renders zo't torat. The LXX renders οὗτος ὁ νόμος ("this is the law") — in agreement, using νόμος ("law") for torah.

20. **"To make a distinction between the unclean and the clean" / le-havdil bein ha-tameh u-vein ha-tahor (v. 47).** Hebrew לְהַבְדִּיל בֵּין הַטָּמֵא וּבֵין הַטָּהוֹר (le-havdil bein ha-tameh u-vein ha-tahor — "to distinguish between the unclean and the clean"). The infinitive le-havdil (hifil of בָּדַל, badal — "to separate, to distinguish") was the key priestly vocation term introduced in Lev 10:10 (where Aaron is charged with distinguishing between the holy and the common, the unclean and the clean). The dietary laws of chapter 11 are here explicitly framed as an instantiation of the priestly havdil function applied to all of Israel: the dietary law is the mechanism by which every Israelite household practices the priestly act of distinguishing clean from unclean. The closing summary of v. 47 thus links chapter 11 back to the priestly vocation established in chapter 10 and forward to the broader holiness theology of the book. "To make a distinction between the unclean and the clean" renders le-havdil bein ha-tameh u-vein ha-tahor precisely. The LXX renders διαστεῖλαι ἀνὰ μέσον τῶν ἀκαθάρτων καὶ ἀνὰ μέσον τῶν καθαρῶν ("to distinguish between the unclean things and the clean things") — in close agreement, using the same διαστέλλω verb as in 10:10.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 95–96) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11:1 | "the LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying to them" | Καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων λέγων πρὸς αὐτούς | LXX agrees precisely with MT, including the dual addressee "to them" (πρὸς αὐτούς for el-hem). |
| 11:2 | "living creatures" (nefesh chayyah) | τὰ ζῷα ("the animals / living beings") | LXX ζῷον for MT nefesh chayyah — LXX uses the generic animal term rather than the "living soul/creature" idiom. |
| 11:5 | shaphan ("rock badger / hyrax") | δασύπουν ("rough-footed / hare") | Significant LXX identification error: LXX renders shaphan as "hare" (δασύπους), distinct from arnevet (the MT's actual word for hare in v. 6). The LXX thus appears to conflate shaphan and arnevet, or reflects a different identification tradition for shaphan in the translators' Egyptian environment, where the hyrax was not known. |
| 11:6 | arnevet ("hare") | χοιρόγρυλλον ("pig-grunter / hedgehog-porcupine") | LXX renders the hare (arnevet) as χοιρόγρυλλον, a different small mammal (the hedgehog or porcupine). Combined with v. 5's rendering of shaphan as "hare," the LXX effectively swaps and displaces the two MT identifications — a notable versional confusion. |
| 11:10 | sheketz ("detestable thing") | βδέλυγμα ("abomination") | LXX consistently renders sheketz as βδέλυγμα throughout the chapter. βδέλυγμα carries somewhat stronger moral-religious connotations in Greek than sheketz does in Hebrew (sheketz is a ritual-taboo term; βδέλυγμα is used for idolatry and moral abomination in the LXX). Slight intensification in the LXX rendering. |
| 11:13 | nesher ("eagle" / possibly vulture) | ἀετόν ("eagle") | LXX agrees with "eagle" identification; some modern scholars prefer "griffon vulture" for nesher in this context. |
| 11:13 | peres ("bearded vulture") | γρύπα ("griffin") | LXX γρύψ ("griffin") for MT peres (bearded vulture) — the griffin is a mythological creature; the LXX translators appear to have equated the large bone-dropping vulture with the legendary griffin or a large raptor known by that name. |
| 11:13 | 'ozniyyah ("black vulture") | ἁλιαίετον ("sea eagle / osprey") | LXX identifies 'ozniyyah as the osprey or sea eagle rather than the black vulture. The identifications of v. 13's three raptors differ substantially between MT and LXX, reflecting the difficulty of cross-cultural bird identification and the different fauna known to Palestinian versus Egyptian scribes. |
| 11:17 | yanshuf ("short-eared owl" / "great owl") | ἶβιν ("ibis") | LXX renders yanshuf as ἶβις — the sacred ibis of Egypt (Threskiornis aethiopicus). This is a striking divergence: the LXX translators apparently associated the yanshuf with the ibis, possibly because the ibis was a prominent Egyptian sacred bird and the LXX was produced in Alexandria. The MT's yanshuf is almost certainly an owl species; the LXX's ἶβις is an entirely different wading bird. |
| 11:18 | tinshmet ("barn owl" — bird form) | πορφυρίωνα ("purple gallinule / water hen") | LXX renders tinshemet (bird) as πορφυρίων — the purple gallinule (Porphyrio porphyrio), a colorful wading bird. The MT tinshemet (bird) is almost certainly an owl; the LXX identification differs completely. Note: tinshemet appears again in v. 30 for a reptile (chameleon), where the LXX renders it ἀσπάλακα ("mole") — again diverging from MT. |
| 11:19 | chasidah ("stork") | ἐρωδιόν ("heron") | LXX renders the stork (chasidah) as heron (ἐρωδιός). Related birds of the same general family (Ciconiiformes), but distinct. The stork has long legs and distinctive black-and-white plumage; the heron is grey. Possibly a genuine faunal ambiguity in the Greek-Egyptian environment. |
| 11:21 | kherayim ("jumping-legs / jointed legs") | σκέλη ἀνώτερα τῶν ποδῶν ("legs higher/above the feet") | LXX renders kherayim as σκέλη ἀνώτερα ("upper/higher legs"), describing the anatomical feature functionally rather than using a technical term. Close agreement on the anatomical description if not on the precise vocabulary. |
| 11:22 | sol'am, chargol, chagav ("bald locust, cricket, grasshopper") | ἀττάκην, ὀφιομάχην, ἀκρίδα | LXX uses various Greek locust and grasshopper terms that do not map precisely to the Hebrew names. The four permitted insect species present identification challenges in both traditions; the LXX attempts different identifications without achieving agreement with the MT terms. |
| 11:33 | "earthen vessel" (keli-cheres) | σκεῦος ὀστράκινον ("earthen/pottery vessel") | Close agreement; LXX ὀστράκινον ("made of pottery/earthenware") for MT cheres ("pottery, earthenware"). |
| 11:36 | ma'yan u-bor mikveh-mayim ("a spring or cistern, a gathering of waters") | πηγὴ ὑδάτων καὶ λάκκος ("a spring of waters and a cistern/pit") | LXX agrees with the distinction between spring (ma'yan / πηγή) and cistern (bor / λάκκος) but renders mikveh-mayim ("gathering of waters") implicitly rather than translating it as a separate noun. |
| 11:43 | teshaqtsevu et-nafshoteikhem ("make your souls/selves detestable") | βδελύξητε τὰς ψυχὰς ὑμῶν ("make your souls an abomination") | LXX agrees on the reflexive construction (nafshoteikhem / τὰς ψυχὰς ὑμῶν — "your souls") and uses βδελύσσω for shaqats. The LXX double negative (οὐ μή + subjunctive) is a strong prohibition marker. |
| 11:44 | ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheikhem ("for I am holy — the LORD your God") | ὅτι ἐγὼ ἅγιος κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν ("for I am holy — the LORD your God") | Close agreement. LXX preserves the "I am holy" formula that serves as the foundation of Israel's holiness calling. |
| 11:45 | he'eleti ("I brought you up") | ὁ ἀναγαγών ("the one who brought up") | LXX uses ἀνάγω ("to lead up / bring up") for MT he'eleti (hifil of 'alah, "to bring up"). Agreement on the specific "bring up" verb rather than the more common "bring out" (hotsi') of the Decalogue formula. |
| 11:47 | le-havdil ("to distinguish / to make a distinction") | διαστεῖλαι ("to distinguish / to separate") | LXX uses the same διαστέλλω verb as in 10:10 (the LXX rendering of havdil) — the verbal correspondence between the priestly vocation of Lev 10:10 and the summary of Lev 11:47 is thus preserved in both MT and LXX. |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 11)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| טָמֵא (tameh) | unclean | Ritual defilement status; LXX: ἀκάθαρτος |
| טָהוֹר (tahor) | clean | Ritual purity status; LXX: καθαρός |
| שֶׁקֶץ (sheketz) | detestable (noun/adj.) | Ritual loathing/taboo; stronger than tameh; LXX: βδέλυγμα |
| שָׁקַץ (shaqats, piel) | regard as detestable / make detestable | Verb of ritual aversion; LXX: βδελύσσω |
| נְבֵלָה (nevelah) | carcass | Dead animal body; carrier of impurity; LXX: τὰ θνησιμαῖα |
| שֶׁרֶץ (sherets) | swarming creature | Teeming/swarming small creatures; LXX: ἑρπετόν |
| פָּרַס (paras, hifil) | part the hoof | Split-hoof criterion; LXX: διχηλεῖν ὁπλήν |
| מַעֲלַת גֵּרָה (ma'alat gerah) | chews the cud | Ruminant criterion; LXX: ἀναμηρυκόμενον |
| סְנַפִּיר (senappir) | fins | Aquatic clean criterion; LXX: πτερύγια |
| קַשְׂקֶשֶׂת (qasqeset) | scales | Aquatic clean criterion; LXX: λεπίδες |
| הַבְדֵּל (havdel, hifil) | make a distinction | Priestly discernment function; LXX: διαστέλλω; links 10:10 and 11:47 |
| תּוֹרָה (torah) | law | Normative instruction; LXX: νόμος |
| נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה (nefesh chayyah) | living creature | Animate life-form; LXX: ζῷον |
| קָדוֹשׁ (qadosh) | holy | Set-apart, sacred; LXX: ἅγιος |
| הִקְדַּשְׁתֶּם (hitqaddishtem) | consecrate yourselves | Reflexive sanctification; LXX: ἁγιάζεσθε |
| גָּמָל (gamal) | camel | LXX: κάμηλον (agreement) |
| שָׁפָן (shaphan) | rock badger | Hyrax; LXX: δασύπουν ("hare" — LXX identification error) |
| אַרְנֶבֶת (arnevet) | hare | LXX: χοιρόγρυλλον (divergent) |
| חֲזִיר (chazir) | pig | LXX: ὗς (agreement) |
| מַעְיָן (ma'yan) | spring | Flowing water source; LXX: πηγή |
| בּוֹר (bor) | cistern | Dug water-holding pit; LXX: λάκκος |
| יהוה | the LORD | YHWH — consistent throughout |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ohel mo'ed) | tent of meeting | LXX: σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου |
| בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (benei Yisrael) | sons of Israel | Retained formal phrase throughout Leviticus |

— LOGOS
